card.what: Mule apps are Maven projects; the mule-maven-plugin handles packaging (mule-application jar) and deployment to every target (CloudHub 1.0/2.0, RTF, on-prem/ARM). Parent POMs + Exchange-as-Maven-repo centralize dependency and plugin governance.
card.how: mvn clean package produces target/{app}-{version}-mule-application.jar. mvn deploy with a configured cloudhub2Deployment/cloudHubDeployment/runtimeFabricDeployment/armDeployment block (or CLI/API) pushes to the target; credentials via connected app (client_id/secret) — never personal accounts in CI. Exchange doubles as a Maven facade for publishing/consuming app artifacts and shared libraries; distributionManagement points at the org's Exchange repo.
exam_traps: One artifact through environments: CI builds ONCE, the SAME jar is promoted dev→test→prod with per-env properties — rebuild-per-env answers are wrong.
Connected apps (client credentials with scoped permissions) are the CI/CD auth standard — username/password (with MFA) breaks automation and fails security review.
skipMunitTests vs skipTests: MUnit binds to Maven's test phase via its own plugin — exams test which flag/phase runs MUnit (mvn clean test runs them; -DskipMunitTests skips).
Version conflicts: connectors are mule-plugins with their own dependencies; the runtime does per-plugin classloading isolation — 'jar hell' answers from plain Java don't map 1:1.
card.hands_on: Take any chapter app: configure the mule-maven-plugin with a cloudhub2Deployment (or cloudHubDeployment) block parameterized by -D properties (env, target, credentials). Run mvn clean package, then mvn deploy -DmuleDeploy with a connected app's credentials. Promote the SAME jar to a second environment by re-running with different properties only.
card.deep_dive:
Settings hygiene: credentials live in settings.xml on the runner (or env vars injected by the CI vault) — never in pom.xml. Parent POM pins runtime version, plugin versions, shared dependencies; org BOM pattern.

Exchange Maven coordinates: groupId = org ID; publishing an app to Exchange enables 'deploy from Exchange' (required model for CH2.0 pipelines).

Classloading: mule-artifact.json + plugin isolation means apps export nothing by default; shared libraries (e.g., JDBC drivers) declared via sharedLibraries in the plugin config — the 'driver class not found' fix exams reference.

quiz.q: Your pipeline rebuilds the app from the same commit for each environment. Architecturally wrong because:
options: Promotes potentially different artifacts (timestamps, resolved deps) — breaks build-once/promote-many traceability
quiz.explanation: Binary provenance: what you tested must be byte-identical to what ships.

quiz.q: CI authenticates to Anypoint for deployments. Correct credential type?
options: A connected app (client credentials) with only the needed scopes
quiz.explanation: Scoped, rotatable, MFA-immune machine identity — the platform's service-account model.

quiz.q: DB connector fails at runtime with 'Cannot load driver class oracle.jdbc.OracleDriver' though the dependency is in the pom. Standard fix?
options: Add the jar to MULE_HOME/lib manually
Declare it under the mule-maven-plugin's sharedLibraries configuration
Shade the driver into the app
quiz.explanation: Plugin classloader isolation requires drivers be exposed as shared libraries to the connector plugin.

card.what: MUnit is Mule's testing framework: XML (or Java) tests running flows in an embedded runtime with mocking (mock-when), spying, assertions (incl. DataWeave matchers), and coverage reporting enforceable as a build gate.
card.how: munit:test wraps Behavior (mock-when on processors selected by name/attributes, returning then-return payload/attributes/variables or raising errors), Execution (flow-ref the flow under test / set-event to seed payload), Validation (assert-that with MunitTools matchers — equalTo, containsString, hasSize; assert-expression). munit-tools::mock-when pins by processor + with-attributes (doc:name etc.). Coverage plugin computes flow/processor coverage with failBuild thresholds in the pom.
exam_traps: mock-when matches the PROCESSOR (e.g., http:request) optionally narrowed by attributes like doc:name — mocking 'the endpoint URL' directly isn't how selection works.
then-error / then-return with an error lets you unit-test error handlers — the way to force HTTP:TIMEOUT without a slow server; candidates often miss that mocks can THROW.
Coverage metrics: application/resource/flow levels with requiredCoverage; runs during mvn test via the munit-maven-plugin — failing the BUILD on low coverage is configuration, not default.
set-event creates the test event (payload/attributes/vars); forgetting attributes when the flow reads attributes.queryParams is the classic 'NPE in test only' bug.
card.hands_on: For your order API: write MUnit tests covering (1) happy path with mocked DB select returning a fixture row, (2) HTTP:CONNECTIVITY raised by a mocked enrichment request asserting your 502 mapping, (3) validation-failure 400 path. Configure coverage failBuild at 80% and run mvn clean test to see the gate in action.
card.deep_dive:
Test design for integration apps: unit-test FLOW LOGIC with all externals mocked; keep a thin set of integration tests against real sandboxes out-of-band (not in every CI run). DW transforms: test pure scripts via dw CLI/Playground cases or MUnit asserting transform output with file fixtures (MunitTools::getResourceAsString).

Spy-when validates calls happened with expected payloads (verify-call times). Parameterized tests via munit:parameterization. Before/After suite/test scopes manage fixtures (e.g., seeding an H2).

Coverage philosophy: 80%+ on process/system APIs realistic; chasing 100% through config-only flows wastes effort — articulate the trade-off, it's an interview/exam essay staple.

quiz.q: How do you unit-test your HTTP:TIMEOUT error mapping without a slow backend?
options: mock-when the http:request processor with then-error (typeId HTTP:TIMEOUT)
Lower the timeout to 1ms in tests
quiz.explanation: Mocks can raise typed errors — deterministic, fast, exactly the path under test.

quiz.q: Which MUnit element seeds the flow-under-test with payload AND queryParams attributes?
options: munit:set-event with payload and attributes values
quiz.explanation: set-event builds the full test event; flows reading attributes need them provided.

quiz.q: Where is the MUnit coverage gate (fail under 80%) configured?
options: munit-maven-plugin configuration (coverage → failBuild + requiredApplicationCoverage) in the pom
quiz.explanation: Coverage enforcement is a build-plugin concern, wired into mvn test.

card.what: A Mule CI/CD pipeline: commit → build+MUnit (+coverage gate) → package once → publish artifact (Exchange) → deploy dev → automated tests → promote (same artifact) through test/prod with approvals. Tooling: mule-maven-plugin, Anypoint CLI v4, and Platform APIs — runnable from Jenkins/GitHub Actions/GitLab/Azure DevOps.
card.how: Anypoint CLI (anypoint-cli-v4) scripts what the UI does: runtime-mgr application deploy/modify, api-mgr api manage/policy apply, exchange asset upload, governance evaluations. Auth via connected app env vars. Typical GitHub Actions: jobs build→test→deploy with environments+secrets, deployment step calling mvn deploy -DmuleDeploy or CLI; API Manager config-as-code applied per environment (apiId resolution scripted).
exam_traps: Pipeline must also promote API MANAGER state (policies, SLA tiers, alerts) — code-only pipelines that hand-configure prod policies fail the 'fully automated' requirement; CLI/Platform APIs/automated policies are the answers.
Secrets in pipelines: CI vault/secret store → env vars → settings.xml/CLI flags; any answer committing credentials or using personal tokens is wrong.
Rollback strategy: redeploy previous artifact version (immutable artifacts make this trivial) — 'fix forward only' or rebuilding old commits are weaker answers.
Environment-specific values arrive at DEPLOY time (deployment properties / secure props), not via per-env builds or branches.
card.hands_on: Build a real pipeline (GitHub Actions): on PR → mvn clean test (MUnit + coverage gate); on merge to main → package, publish to Exchange, deploy to a dev environment via connected-app secrets, run a smoke test (curl health endpoint), manual approval job → promote the same artifact to a 'prod' environment. Keep the YAML in the repo.
card.deep_dive:
Config-as-code maturity ladder: (1) scripted app deploys; (2) + API Manager via CLI (api-mgr) or platform APIs idempotently (create-or-update semantics, apiId captured per env); (3) + governance gates (conformance check in PR); (4) + automated post-deploy verification (smoke + contract tests) and auto-rollback on failure.

Branching: trunk-based with short-lived feature branches fits build-once-promote-many; GitFlow's env-branches encourage env-rebuilds (anti-pattern here).

Blue/green & canary on CH2.0/RTF: deploy v2 alongside, shift traffic at the LB/ingress or via API Manager upstream weights (Flex) — recognition level. Nexus/Artifactory as alternative artifact stores when Exchange-publishing isn't the org standard for apps.

quiz.q: Your 'fully automated' pipeline deploys apps but admins click policies into API Manager for prod. Architect's verdict?
options: Gap: API Manager state must be promoted as code (CLI/Platform APIs) for reproducible environments
quiz.explanation: Environment reproducibility includes governance config — UI-only steps are drift generators.

quiz.q: Prod deploy of 2.4.0 goes bad. Fastest safe rollback given immutable artifacts in Exchange?
options: Redeploy the stored 2.3.x artifact (same jar that ran before) with its known config
Git revert and wait for CI
quiz.explanation: Immutable, versioned artifacts exist exactly for instant, provenance-preserving rollback.

quiz.q: Which Anypoint CLI v4 command family manages deployments to CloudHub 2.0?
quiz.explanation: runtime-mgr covers CH2.0/RTF app lifecycle in CLI v4.

concepts.title: Quality Gates: Code Review, Static Analysis & Versioning Discipline

card.what: Engineering hygiene that exams and real orgs grade: semantic versioning of apps/APIs, PR review standards for Mule XML/DW, static analysis (governance rulesets, custom linting), dependency/runtime upgrade strategy, and documentation-as-code.
card.how: Semver: MAJOR breaking API/behavior, MINOR features, PATCH fixes — app version in pom, API major in URL/instance. Reviews check: flow size/naming, error handling completeness, externalized config, DW readability (no mega-scripts), test coverage deltas. Runtime upgrades: track LTS/Edge channels (Java 17 era runtimes), test in lower envs, leverage backward compatibility of apps. Mule LTS vs Edge release cadence governs upgrade planning.
exam_traps: API version lives in the URL/instance for MAJOR only (v1, v2) — minor/patch must be non-breaking and invisible to consumers; '/v1.2/' in a path is an anti-pattern answer.
Runtime upgrade questions: apps declare minMuleVersion; patch updates on CloudHub can be platform-applied — pinning forever is the wrong posture; testing-then-promote is right.
Deprecation lifecycle for APIs: announce → parallel-run old+new majors → consumer migration tracking via contracts/analytics → retire. Skipping parallel-run is the trap.
Naming conventions (kebab-case assets, sys-/prc-/exp- prefixes) are governance signals — recognized in spec/asset questions.
card.hands_on: Write your team's one-page Mule engineering standard: naming, flow size limits, mandatory error contract, DW style (module reuse, no inline >15 lines), test coverage floor, versioning rules, upgrade cadence. Apply it as a PR checklist to one of your quest repos and review a past chapter's app against it honestly.
card.deep_dive:
Version compatibility matrix thinking: app ↔ runtime (minMuleVersion), connector ↔ runtime, API spec ↔ implementation ↔ instances per environment — drift across any axis creates the subtle prod-only bugs. Track in a simple manifest.

Deprecation telemetry: API Manager contracts + analytics tell you WHO still calls v1 — data-driven retirement, not guesswork. Consumer notification via Exchange portal announcements.

Docs-as-code: README per app (purpose, contracts, SLOs, runbooks), Exchange asset pages generated from specs, ADRs (architecture decision records) for the big calls — the artifacts MCIA scenario answers should reference when asked 'how do you ensure maintainability'.

quiz.q: You must add an optional response field to a v1 API. Correct version action?
options: MINOR bump of the spec/app — additive optional changes are non-breaking within v1
Breaking — remove the old field too
quiz.explanation: Additive+optional = backward compatible = minor. Majors are reserved for breaks.

quiz.q: How do you decide when v1 can be retired after launching v2?
options: When API Manager contracts/analytics show v1 consumers migrated (or formally exempted)
quiz.explanation: Retirement is evidence-driven via consumer telemetry, not calendar-driven.

quiz.q: A PR adds a 600-line flow with inline 80-line DW and on-error-continue around everything. Primary review verdict?
options: Reject: decompose flows, extract DW to modules/files, and replace blanket continue with typed handlers — maintainability and correctness risks
quiz.explanation: Blanket error-swallowing is a correctness bug; monolith flows and inline mega-DW are maintainability debt. Reviews exist for exactly this.

concepts.title: MUnit in Practice: XML Test Architecture

card.what: Concrete MUnit XML structure: munit:test wraps three phases — munit:behavior (mock-when), munit:execution (flow-ref), munit:validation (assert-that). Each mock pins a processor by type + doc:name attribute; then-return supplies fixture payload; assert-that uses MunitTools matchers.
card.how: Pattern: mock-when processor="db:select" with-attribute name="doc:name" value="Query Legacy DB" → then-return payload as application/java list. flow-ref executes the flow under test with that mock in place. assert-that checks expression #[payload.message] is equalTo('Event received successfully'). For error paths: use then-raise instead of then-return to inject HTTP:TIMEOUT, then assert the error handler's output.
exam_traps: mock-when matches by PROCESSOR TYPE (db:select, http:request) — not by URL or SQL query. Narrowing by doc:name is optional but prevents wrong-mock collisions when a flow has two DB calls.

then-return mediaType must match what the flow expects downstream — application/java for Java collections (DB result sets), application/json for parsed maps. Wrong mediaType = type mismatch error in the mocked flow.
verify-call (not assert-that) checks whether a processor was called N times — use it to assert a Salesforce upsert ran exactly once, not zero. Common exam swap.
set-event seeds the test's inbound Mule Event (payload + attributes + vars) before execution — skipping it when the flow reads attributes.queryParams.status causes NullPointerException in test only.
card.hands_on: Write three MUnit tests for a customer GET flow: (1) mock db:select → returns [{id:'001',name:'Arjun'}], assert payload[0].name equals 'Arjun'; (2) mock db:select → then-raise DB:CONNECTIVITY, assert error handler sets HTTP status 503; (3) use verify-call to assert an audit logger flow-ref fired exactly once on success. Run mvn clean test and confirm all three pass.
card.deep_dive:
Full XML anatomy:
<munit:test name="getCustomerFlow-success">
  <munit:behavior>
    <munit-tools:mock-when processor="db:select">
      <munit-tools:with-attributes>
        <munit-tools:with-attribute name="doc:name" value="Query Legacy DB"/>
      </munit-tools:with-attributes>
      <munit-tools:then-return>
        <munit-tools:payload value="#[[{id:'001',name:'Arjun'}]]" mediaType="application/java"/>
      </munit-tools:then-return>
    </munit-tools:mock-when>
  </munit:behavior>
  <munit:execution>
    <flow-ref name="getCustomerFlow"/>
  </munit:execution>
  <munit:validation>
    <munit-tools:assert-that expression="#[payload.message]" is="#[MunitTools::equalTo('Event received successfully')]"/>
  </munit:validation>
</munit:test>

Test naming convention: {flowName}-{scenario}-test. Suite-level before/after for DB seed/teardown. Coverage: munit-coverage-report plugin with applicationCoverageFlowThreshold=80 in pom.xml.

quiz.q: You have two db:select processors in one flow. How do you ensure only the first one is mocked?
options: Mock both with the same payload
Narrow mock-when with a with-attribute on doc:name matching only the first processor's name
Use flow-ref to mock individual steps
quiz.explanation: with-attribute on doc:name (or any XML attribute) scopes the mock to that specific processor instance.

quiz.q: How do you test that your error handler correctly handles DB:CONNECTIVITY in MUnit?
options: Use then-raise inside mock-when to inject DB:CONNECTIVITY, then assert-that on the error handler's output payload
You can't test error paths with MUnit
quiz.explanation: then-raise lets mocks inject any error type without needing a real failure condition.

quiz.q: Which MUnit tool verifies a Salesforce connector fired exactly once during a flow execution?
quiz.explanation: verify-call is the execution-count assertion tool; assert-that is for payload/value matching.

side_quests.title: Pipeline of Steel
side_quests.description: Production-grade GitHub Actions (or Jenkins) pipeline for one quest app: PR → MUnit + 80% coverage gate + governance conformance check; main → package once, publish to Exchange, deploy dev, smoke test, manual approval, promote SAME artifact to prod env, post-deploy verification, auto-rollback step on smoke failure. All secrets via the CI secret store.

side_quests.title: The Coverage Reckoning
side_quests.description: Take your Chapter 6 payment-reconciler and drive MUnit coverage ≥85% honestly: error paths, poison path, batch on-complete, idempotency branch. No assertion-free tests. Record which bugs the tests actually caught — there will be at least one.

boss.title: The Pipeline Tyrant
boss.intro: The Pipeline Tyrant has zero tolerance: untested code doesn't merge, unversioned artifacts don't deploy, and anyone clicking prod configs by hand answers to him directly.

mcq.q: Order the correct pipeline: (a) deploy dev (b) MUnit+coverage (c) package (d) promote same artifact (e) publish to Exchange (f) smoke tests (g) approval.
mcq.explanation: Test → package once → publish → deploy dev → verify → gate → promote. Build-once is the spine.

mcq.q: MUnit test needs the flow to receive an HTTP 404 from a mocked downstream and assert your APP:CUSTOMER_MISSING mapping. Correct mock?
options: mock-when http:request → then-error typeId HTTP:NOT_FOUND (your error-mapping transforms it)
mcq.explanation: Status-as-payload doesn't traverse the error path; raising the typed error exercises mapping + handler.

mcq.q: Two teams' apps break only in prod: team A built per-env jars; team B's pipeline deploys API policies via UI notes. Root causes in one phrase each?
options: Artifact drift (no build-once) / governance config drift (no config-as-code)
mcq.explanation: Both are reproducibility failures — binary and configuration respectively.

mcq.q: Which coverage approach earns the Tyrant's respect?
options: 80%+ gate with meaningful assertions, all error paths tested via then-error, coverage diff visible in PRs
Only happy paths, prod will find the rest
mcq.explanation: Gates + assertion quality + error-path coverage — the trio that actually prevents regressions.

mcq.q: Prod incident: yesterday's deploy must go back NOW, and the bad version must be reproducible for forensics. Your pipeline enables this because:
options: Every deploy references an immutable Exchange artifact version + captured deployment config — redeploy N-1, quarantine N for analysis
Prod has source code on disk
mcq.explanation: Immutable artifacts + recorded config = instant rollback + forensic reproducibility.

scenario.prompt:
BUILD TASK — Serve the Tyrant:

Deliver the full DevOps treatment to one existing quest app:
1. MUnit suite: happy, 4xx, 5xx/timeout, idempotency-duplicate paths; coverage gate ≥80% enforced in mvn test.
2. CI pipeline file in-repo implementing build-once/promote-many with connected-app secrets.
3. API Manager config-as-code script (CLI or platform API calls) applying policies+SLA tiers idempotently to dev.
4. Version + rollback drill: ship 1.1.0, then roll back to 1.0.x by artifact reference, timing the operation.
5. A one-page engineering standard (naming, error contract, coverage floor, versioning) committed as STANDARDS.md.
checklist: Coverage gate demonstrably fails the build when a test is removed
Same artifact hash deployed to both environments (prove it)
Policy application script is rerunnable without duplicating config
Rollback completed and timed; forensics artifact preserved
STANDARDS.md applied retroactively: one real refactor committed because of it
